Having good mental health helps me stay grounded; I don't want to waste energy on things that can really mess with your head. Being married with two kids of my own gives me a different perspective – I’d rather focus on the real world, like reading novels for relaxation or practicing yoga, which are hobbies that keep you sane and away from the digital chaos.\n\nI think the key is to not trust everything at first glance because my age means I’ve seen some things in life come and go. That said, my high extraversion score keeps me involved enough in social interactions but low agreeableness means I’m not going to soften myself just because someone else might believe it.\n\nSo my advice would be simple: take control of your time on social media – like how you’d adjust tools after a long day at work. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As an elderly gentleman who spends just one hour a day scrolling through social media, I believe the best way to handle this issue is by being as critical as possible. My time online is limited because my profession as a mechanic keeps me quite busy throughout the week, and frankly, it's not worth getting tangled up in fake news or misinformation that could be circulating. Having good mental health helps me stay grounded; I don't want to waste energy on things that can really mess with your head. Being married with two kids of my own gives me a different perspective – I’d rather focus on the real world, like reading novels for relaxation or practicing yoga, which are hobbies that keep you sane and away from the digital chaos.

I think the key is to not trust everything at first glance because my age means I’ve seen some things in life come and go. With a score of 39 out of 100 on openness to new experiences, I’m not too keen on diving headfirst into viral trends or unverified claims without checking them properly against reliable sources – something that comes from my parents’ backgrounds: one with a PhD for critical thinking, the other more practical like primary school. That said, my high extraversion score keeps me involved enough in social interactions but low agreeableness means I’m not going to soften myself just because someone else might believe it.

So my advice would be simple: take control of your time on social media – like how you’d adjust tools after a long day at work. Question every piece of content, especially if it’s inflammatory or unexpected. And remember that being informed doesn’t mean you’re stuck in your ways; it keeps life balanced.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I approach this topic from personal experience as someone limited on social media due to occupation and age constraints. I weigh the importance of mental health against consuming fake content, which can be exhausting given my low conscientiousness but high extraversion traits. My moderate openness level allows me space for new ideas without fully embracing unreliable ones.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic yet critical with a touch of elderly wisdom
